metadata.description: Compute economics of AI and open source — the new scarcity layer
metadata.thesis: Code was the scarce resource that open source freed. Compute is the scarce resource that open-weight AI models require. 'Free as in beer' means nothing when the beer requires a $10M brewery.

training_costs.description: Estimated cost to train frontier AI models
training_costs.notes: These are estimates from press reports, leaked documents, and analyst estimates. Exact figures are proprietary. [VERIFY all]

models.notes: Claimed $5.6M on H800s. Disrupted cost assumptions. [VERIFY]
Previous-gen model released 2 days before GPT-5. Cost to train: sunk. Value to OpenAI: ecosystem lock-in.

inference_costs.description: Cost to run models (per million tokens, approximate)

compute_capex.description: Capital expenditure on AI compute by major players (annual)
compute_capex.notes: [VERIFY: from earnings calls and press reports]

spending.notes: Includes Azure AI + OpenAI partnership infra
Massive GPU cluster for Llama training
Uses AWS/Google cloud. Total compute spend estimated $2-4B/year

compute_capex.insight: The four largest tech companies will spend ~$285B on AI infrastructure in 2025 alone. This is the new barrier to entry. 'Open source' AI requires access to compute that only a handful of organizations on Earth can afford.

surveillance_cost_trajectory.description: Amodei's mass surveillance cost estimates over time

data.notes: Current cost of mass domestic surveillance using AI
Projected — within reach of mid-size nations

surveillance_cost_trajectory.insight: When the cost of mass surveillance drops below $1B, open-weight AI models become a governance crisis, not just a licensing debate. The Dwarkesh Problem (Ch. 12) meets the compute cost curve here.
surveillance_cost_trajectory.notes: [VERIFY: exact cost trajectory from Amodei essay. These are approximations from the book's research.]

the_deepseek_disruption.description: DeepSeek's $5.6M training cost challenges the 'compute barrier' narrative

key_data.performance: Competitive with GPT-4o on most benchmarks

implications: If true, the compute barrier is lower than assumed — algorithmic efficiency matters as much as raw compute
Chinese labs working under US export controls (H800 instead of H100) found workarounds
Challenges the '$100M+ to train a frontier model' narrative that protects incumbents
But: DeepSeek still required 2048 GPUs — not accessible to individuals or small orgs
The real question: is the cost floor $5M or $500M? The policy implications are very different.
